report: Initial tonic stiffening involving the upper body, with right arm flexion at the elbow and wrist and extension of the left arm, accompanied by head turning to the right; followed by bilateral clonic jerking of both arms and trunk, with asynchronous flexion-extension movements of the arms and legs. The head remains midline after initial deviation, and no facial automatisms are observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.
Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm flexion; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Initial subtle head turning to the left with left arm flexion and right arm extension, progressing to generalized tonic stiffening followed by bilateral clonic jerking; initial tonic stiffening of the left arm with extension also observed, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking of the left upper extremity that later evolves to involve both arms and head. Patient remains unresponsive throughout with eyes open, fixed gaze, and postictal unresponsiveness characterized by arms extended and head tilted back.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient lies supine with minimal arm movements, including brief right arm extension and hand gestures, and occasional head turning. No tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms are evident.

Initial subtle head turning to the right with right arm extension; progresses to rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the right arm and hand, with occasional upward arm movement and facial grimacing. No generalized tonic–clonic activity observed.
Blank stare with upward eye deviation; right arm flexion followed by bilateral arm flexion and rhythmic jerking of limbs. No head version or automatisms observed.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation; right arm extends and left arm flexes (fencer posture), followed by bilateral arm extension and rhythmic jerking. Mouth opens and closes rhythmically during the event.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by head tilt to the left and subtle left arm flexion; gradual progression to generalized tonic stiffening with bilateral limb rigidity and rhythmic clonic jerks.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains alert, with eye movements and facial expressions consistent with wakefulness. Brief hand movements occur while interacting with a laptop, including opening the device, typing, and pointing, without signs of automatisms or motor seizures.
Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks.
Blank stare with subtle rightward head version; followed by tonic stiffening of the left arm and then bilateral tonic–clonic movements.
Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture) develop, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks in both limbs.
Blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture); progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks of limbs and head.
Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks of arms and legs.

Blank stare with upward gaze deviation; right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posturing) develop, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms. Patient remains unresponsive with eyes open and head tilted back.

Blank stare with variable head version (rightward or leftward) and corresponding asymmetric arm movements, including right or left arm flexion or extension, may occur initially. Fencer posture (right arm flexion with left arm extension) is observed in some episodes. These features evolve into bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs, with possible automatisms such as lip smacking, tongue thrusting, mouth opening with frothing, and persistent eye deviation in the direction of initial head version. The patient remains unresponsive during these events. In contrast, at other times, no overt seizure activity is observed; the patient remains awake, alert, and without any clinical signs such as blank stare, automatisms, tonic-clonic movements, head version, fencer posturing, or asymmetric motor activity.

Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version, upward eye deviation, and tonic stiffening involving the right arm and left leg; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with asynchronous rhythmic clonic jerks in all limbs. Brief facial grimacing and tongue protrusion observed during peak activity.

Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension with left arm flexion (fencer posture); progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Patient remains unresponsive with eyes closed throughout, culminating in postictal unresponsiveness.

Initial subtle head turning to the right with mild right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral arm jerking and generalized tonic–clonic movements; also noted is a blank stare with rightward head version, right arm extension, left arm flexion, facial twitching, and eye deviation. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Patient exhibits brief head turning to the left, followed by subtle right arm flexion and left arm extension. No overt clonic movements or tonic stiffening observed. Patient remains seated and appears to regain awareness shortly after.
No seizure activity observed. Patient remains alert and responsive, with subtle hand movements and facial expression changes during interaction. No tonic, clonic, or automatisms noted.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient remains alert and responsive, with occasional hand movements and facial expressions consistent with conversation. No tonic, clonic, or automatisms are evident.
No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient is awake, holding a can, and drinking from it. He later uses a tube-like object near his chest while remaining alert and responsive.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient is awake, alert, and manually adjusting a medical device on his chest with both hands. There is no tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, automatisms, or altered consciousness.
Blank stare with mild facial grimacing; subtle rhythmic jerking of the right hand and arm, progressing to brief generalized jerking. No clear tonic stiffening or fencer posture observed.

No overt seizure activity is observed in the initial segments; the patient remains awake and alert or supine with eyes open or closed, holding a telephone receiver or remote control, and exhibits minimal to no facial or limb movements. There is no head version, facial automatisms, limb stiffening, tonic posturing, or clonic jerking during these periods. Respiratory and cardiac monitoring devices are visible, but no clinical signs of seizure are present. In one segment, a blank stare with mild jaw opening occurs, followed by rhythmic jerking of the right arm and hand, without spread to other limbs or generalized clonic activity; the patient becomes unresponsive with eyes closed during this event.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient is awake, holding a white handheld device, and intermittently bringing it to his mouth. He exhibits no facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or abnormal posturing.

No overt seizure activity is observed throughout the observation period. The patient is awake, alert, and responsive, with preserved verbal communication evidenced by speaking or attempting to speak, as well as facial expressions and hand gestures consistent with interaction. Occasional hand movements include lifting, tapping, and placement on the chest, with no tonic or clonic limb movements, head deviation, automatisms, or limb asymmetry noted. The patient remains supine with eyes open and mouth slightly agape at times, exhibiting intermittent eye, mouth, and arm movements. There is no blank stare, unresponsiveness, tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, or automatisms during periods of observation. The patient is observed interacting with a tablet device, further indicating sustained alertness and engagement.

No overt motor seizures or seizure activity observed; patient remains supine, awake, with eyes open and mouth slightly agape throughout. There is no tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, head version, or automatisms. Minimal facial movement is noted, including occasional mouth opening and closing, as well as intermittent head movement. The patient uses a tissue to wipe his face and interacts with a remote control device on his chest, demonstrating limited purposeful motor activity.

- Patient exhibits a blank stare with mild facial twitching, followed by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the right arm and shoulder that spreads to the left arm and trunk; no tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, head version, or posturing observed. - During periods without overt seizure activity, patient remains alert and responsive, with only minimal movement such as hand gestures and slight head motion; no automatisms or lateralizing features present. - Episodes also include fixed upward gaze with mouth open and jaw slightly agape, accompanied by unresponsiveness and sustained facial expression; no limb movements, automatisms, tonic-clonic activity, or lateralizing signs observed during these events.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with subtle lip smacking and a fixed, unblinking gaze. Right arm flexion is followed by left arm extension (fencer posture), then rightward head version and right arm extension. Generalized tonic stiffening occurs with rhythmic clonic jerks; no automatisms are observed. No overt motor activity or limb posturing is noted beyond these episodes, and the patient remains immobile and unresponsive throughout.

No observable seizure activity initially; patient remains motionless with eyes closed, no facial automatisms, no limb movements, and no tonic or clonic phases noted. Subsequently, an episode begins with a blank stare and upward eye deviation, followed by tonic stiffening of the body and arms, progressing to bilateral clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements.

Initial blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic jerking and asynchronous clonic movements involving both upper limbs and trunk, culminating in sustained posturing with open mouth and tongue protrusion.

Blank stare accompanied by head version (to the right or left) and asymmetric limb posturing, including right arm extension with contralateral arm flexion consistent with a fencer posture, is observed. Initial tonic stiffening involves the bilateral limbs and trunk, often with arms flexed tightly across the chest or unilateral arm extension, followed by progression to bilateral clonic jerking of the arms, legs, and trunk. Clonic activity may begin focally (e.g., in the right arm and leg) before generalizing. Head turning is commonly directed to the right, though leftward turning also occurs, frequently associated with eye deviation, which may be brief and upward. Orofacial automatisms include lip smacking, tongue thrusting, jaw clenching, and sustained mouth opening with tongue protrusion. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, which evolve from focal onset to bilateral tonic–clonic motor activity.

Initial blank stare with subtle rightward head turning and right arm extension, accompanied by left arm flexion (fencer posture), progresses to generalized tonic stiffening with bilateral clonic jerks involving all limbs. The event concludes with sustained tonic posturing and diminished movement.

Blank stare with subtle rightward head version progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs; initial tonic stiffening involves the arms, with sustained extension of the right arm and flexion of the left, while head deviation persists to the right; automatisms include mouth opening and tongue thrusting; shaking becomes symmetrically rhythmic.

No overt seizure activity observed during periods of baseline monitoring. Patient remains seated with eyes open, hands resting in lap, and no facial automatisms, limb jerking, tonic posturing, or head version noted. Episodes characterized by blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, evolving into bilateral arm involvement with tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks. Progression to generalized tonic–clonic movements observed in one episode, during which the patient remained unresponsive with eyes closed and hands covering face. Lips remained closed throughout all ictal events.

Blank stare with brief loss of eye contact and mouth slightly open, accompanied by subtle rightward head version; right arm flexion progressing to sustained tonic posturing and rhythmic clonic jerking, which spreads to the left arm and legs. Left arm remains relatively still initially, but later involved in clonic activity.
Blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks.

Blank stare with downward or mild eye deviation and head turning or deviation to the right, followed by right arm flexion or extension and contralateral (left arm) flexion. This progresses to unilateral tonic stiffening of the upper body and arms, involving the right side initially, then spreading to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerking that begins in the right arm and hand and evolves into generalized bilateral clonic movements. The episode concludes with sustained tonic posturing and persistent unresponsiveness throughout.

Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, evolving to rhythmic jerking of the right arm and hand with sustained right hand clenching and intermittent right facial twitching; no overt seizure activity is observed. The patient remains seated with a neutral facial expression, holding his head with one hand, and making minor movements of the head and eyes. There is no tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, or automatisms.

No overt seizure activity is observed throughout the recording. The patient is alert, sitting upright in bed, and engaging in conversation using hand gestures. He drinks from a cup, places it down, and repeatedly clasps and unclasps his hands while speaking. There is no blank stare, automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, head version, or any change in posture or motor behavior. Occasional finger movements and shifts in gaze are noted, but no abnormal motor activity is present.

Blank stare with variable head version (leftward and rightward); right arm flexion followed by bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks. Automatisms including lip smacking and hand-to-mouth movements observed during some episodes.

Initial blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by sudden head tilt to the right with right arm extension and left arm flexion. Generalized tonic stiffening ensues, with bilateral arm flexion and extension, mouth opening widely, and tongue protrusion during the tonic phase. Rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs occur, with head and eyes deviated upward; no automatisms observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, and rhythmic jerking persists for several seconds.

Blank stare followed by upward eye deviation, bilateral tonic stiffening of the arms and legs, and arm elevation with flexion; progresses to rhythmic clonic jerking of all limbs and trunk, with occasional head turning to the right. Mouth opens widely with tongue protrusion and frothing at the lips.

Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks involving arms and trunk, then progresses to asynchronous limb movements and vocalization.

The patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the video, exhibiting no overt motor activity, automatisms, or changes in facial expression. There is no observable tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, head version, or limb movements. The patient appears unresponsive with consistent, shallow breathing and no signs of seizure evolution. Position is supine with hands resting on the abdomen and head in neutral position. Occasional subtle hand shifts and a slight head tilt are noted, along with brief bilateral hand movements near the end involving slight finger extension and flexion under the blanket. In one segment, the patient has eyes open with a blank stare and demonstrates unresponsiveness, subtle lip smacking, and mild right arm flexion; however, no overt tonic or clonic movements are observed in any segment.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with rightward head version and initial asymmetric motor features, including right arm extension and either contralateral arm flexion or extension (fencer posture). This progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes open and a fixed gaze. Automatisms and oral movements are not observed.

Blank stare with head version and corresponding arm movements—rightward head turn with right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), and leftward head turn with left arm flexion—progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs. Unresponsive with eyes closed throughout the event.

Blank stare accompanied by head version (left or right) and corresponding arm movements—either left arm flexion with right arm extension (fencer posture) or right arm extension—progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Automatisms such as lip smacking are observed during some episodes. Eye deviation occurs briefly, and shaking is consistently rhythmic.

Initial blank stare with head version (right or left) and corresponding arm posturing—right arm extension or left arm flexion—followed by bilateral tonic stiffening, then evolution into generalized rhythmic clonic jerks. Episodes progress to sustained tonic posture with unresponsiveness throughout.

Blank stare with subtle right arm flexion and left arm extension; minimal rhythmic jerking in right hand, followed by sustained right arm flexion and left arm extension. No overt clonic movements or automatisms observed.

Blank stare with subtle lip smacking and rightward head version, followed by development of right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), right arm extension, bilateral tonic stiffening, and rhythmic clonic jerks in both limbs.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation and subtle rightward head version, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture). Progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks involving all four limbs, including prominent rhythmic jerking of the right arm. Concludes with sustained tonic posture and minimal movement.

Initial blank stare with head version (leftward and rightward), accompanied by asymmetric motor signs including right arm flexion, left arm extension (fencer posture), and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by asynchronous clonic jerks and rhythmic limb jerking with intermittent arm elevation. Automatisms include lip smacking and tongue thrusting.

No overt seizure activity observed initially; patient remains seated, alert, and responsive throughout early segments, with eyes open and directed to the right, no observable head version, limb movement, or facial automatisms, and hands resting on the lap in a static posture. Subsequent evolution includes blank stare accompanied by subtle head turning to the right and subtle lip smacking. This progresses to right arm flexion followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Further development involves bilateral arm movements, generalized tonic–clonic activity, and bilateral tonic stiffening with asynchronous clonic jerks involving arms and legs. Tonic stiffening of the body with arms extended occurs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking of limbs and trunk, including head shaking. Facial grimacing and tongue thrusting are noted, with eyes remaining open—initially deviated to the right, then becoming fixed and upward. Automatisms include finger movements over the food tray, progressing to uncoordinated arm movements and partial body stiffening. The episode culminates in generalized convulsive activity involving trunk and limbs, followed by postictal unresponsiveness and slumped posture. No further automatisms or focal motor signs are observed during the final phase.

No overt seizure activity observed; patient remains alert and responsive throughout, in a supine position with eyes open, hands clasped, and head in neutral position. No blank stare, facial or manual automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or posturing noted. Minimal hand movement is consistent with non-epileptic behavior. Respiratory and cardiac monitors show stable rhythms without abrupt changes.

Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; sudden bilateral tonic stiffening of the upper limbs progresses to involve the entire body, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking of arms and legs. No focal motor signs or automatisms observed. Eyes remain open with a fixed gaze throughout the episode.

Blank stare with subtle eye deviation to the right, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking of arms and legs with sustained tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. Movements are symmetric and generalized throughout the episode; no clear head version or automatisms observed.

Initial blank stare with subtle lip smacking, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening, then rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs, evolving into asynchronous clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. Ends with sustained bilateral clonic movements and unresponsiveness.

- Blank stare with subtle lip smacking and mild rightward head version, with or without right arm flexion or extension (including fencer posturing); may progress to bilateral tonic stiffening or posturing involving limbs and trunk, with eyes deviated upward and unresponsiveness to external stimuli. - Evolution to rhythmic clonic jerks in both arms and legs, which may become asynchronous; episodes may begin with upward gaze and bilateral arm movements including flexion and extension. - No overt seizure activity is observed during other periods: patient remains seated upright, focused on a handheld device near the face or mouth, with only occasional hand movements, minimal movement, or slight head tilting; no blank stare, automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, posturing, lateralized or generalized motor signs, or altered awareness are present.

Initial tonic stiffening of the body with head turned to the right, followed by bilateral clonic jerking of the arms and legs with asynchronous limb movements. The episode begins with a blank stare and upward gaze, progressing to tonic stiffening of both arms and legs. Automatisms include lip smacking and chewing movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout with eyes closed.

Blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to tonic stiffening of the right arm with flexion at the elbow, followed by asynchronous jerking movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with upward deviation of the eyes and sustained head tilt backward, remaining supine with eyes open and fixed in upward gaze throughout. The patient is motionless with arms at sides, showing no response to external stimuli. No automatisms, head version, or asymmetric limb posturing are observed. While most episodes lack overt motor activity, one segment includes subtle rhythmic jerking of the right arm. In another, tonic stiffening of the body occurs, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs with asynchronous movements. Otherwise, no tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, facial expression changes, or limb position changes are noted across the observation period.

- Patient exhibits a blank stare with rightward head version and either right arm extension or flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic or asynchronous clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. No automatisms or vocalizations are observed, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout these episodes. - No overt seizure activity is observed in some segments. The patient remains alert, interactive, and seated, with no blank stare, automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, posturing, or head version. There is no evidence of altered consciousness or motor seizure manifestations. In other instances without seizure activity, the patient remains in a resting posture with eyes closed, making minimal movements such as occasional gaze shifts or small hand motions, but without any facial automatisms, limb movements, or epileptic features.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by rightward head version and asymmetric motor activity including right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture). This evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening, then rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, progressing to generalized tonic–clonic movements affecting limbs and trunk. In some episodes, the initial focal features involve tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking of the right arm and leg before generalization. Eyes remain open and fixed upward during motor events, with no facial automatisms, lip smacking, or additional automatisms observed. Movements are sustained and symmetric during generalized phases. In contrast, other observations show the patient remaining supine with eyes closed throughout, demonstrating no overt motor activity or change in posture. No blank stare, head deviation, limb movements, tonic-clonic activity, asymmetric posturing, or automatisms are present. Respiratory pattern remains stable without signs of airway compromise, and no early clinical signs are evident in these episodes.

Patient remains supine throughout the observation, with intermittent periods of eyes open and fixed upward or eyes closed, accompanied by head tilted back. No overt motor activity, facial movements, limb jerking, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, focal motor signs, automatisms, or changes in facial expression or respiratory pattern are observed during the majority of the recording. There is no evidence of head version, asymmetric limb posturing, lateralizing signs, or posturing during these periods. However, at one point, the patient exhibits tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, followed by bilateral, generalized rhythmic clonic jerking involving the arms and legs, without clear focal onset or lateralization. The patient remains unresponsive during this episode, with eyes deviated upward.

Patient exhibits a sudden blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening, then asymmetric clonic jerking with more pronounced movements on the right side; rhythmic clonic jerks involve both arms and legs. Automatisms are not observed, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient remains supine with eyes closed, exhibiting no facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or posturing. No head version or limb movements are noted throughout.

Patient exhibits tonic stiffening with left arm flexion and right arm extension (fencer posture), followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both limbs. Head remains turned to the right throughout. No automatisms or eye movements observed. Subsequently, no overt seizure activity is observed; patient lies on side, motionless, with no facial or limb movements, no eye deviation, no automatisms, and no tonic or clonic features. No change in posture or behavior throughout this period.

- Patient exhibits a blank stare, often with head tilt (backward or rightward) and eye deviation (upward or rightward), accompanied by asymmetric arm posturing: right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture). - This posturing may persist without evolution into clonic activity, or progress to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking involving the arms and face. - Clonic movements are sometimes asynchronous and may include facial components such as mouth opening and tongue protrusion (automatisms); these automatisms are not always present. - Episodes may involve bilateral tonic stiffening of the neck, upper body, and limbs, followed by clonic jerking. - In some instances, the episode remains non-convulsive, with sustained asymmetric limb posturing and no clonic, tonic, or automotor features. - The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no vocalizations observed.

- Initial blank stare, often with eye closure and head tilted back, occasionally accompanied by upward eye deviation; variable presence of rightward or leftward head version. - Rightward head version associated with right arm extension or flexion, progressing to tonic stiffening of the right shoulder and upper arm, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the right arm in some episodes; one episode features left arm tonic stiffening with clonic jerking of the left arm and shoulder, and sustained leftward head turn. - Bilateral tonic stiffening observed in some segments, evolving into generalized rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs; however, other episodes show no spread beyond unilateral involvement and lack generalization. - Automatisms such as lip smacking and tongue thrusting present in one segment; absent in others. - Episodes may include sustained posturing without clonic movements—such as extended and slightly elevated right arm with contralateral elbow flexion—or complete motor arrest with no observable movement, tonic activity, clonic jerks, automatisms, or postural changes. - Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with motionless body posture and no change in limb position in certain segments; observed in supine position during inactive episodes.

No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient remains supine with eyes closed, arms resting by the sides, and no facial or limb movements suggestive of tonic, clonic, or automatisms. No head version or posturing is noted.

Patient lies supine with eyes closed, exhibiting no overt motor activity. No early signs such as blank stare, lip smacking, or automatisms are observed initially, and no tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or lateralized movements occur during the early phase. The patient remains in this state without head version or limb asymmetry until a sudden onset of blank stare develops, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This evolves into generalized tonic stiffening, then bilateral clonic jerking of the limbs with asynchronous movement. No vocalizations are noted during the event.

No overt seizure activity is observed, though the patient remains unresponsive throughout with eyes closed or open and head tilted back. A blank stare with upward eye deviation occurs, followed by development of right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), along with subtle rhythmic jerking of the right hand and arm. This progresses to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. There is no tonic stiffening or automatisms, and the left arm remains still during initial focal activity.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by rhythmic jerking of the right arm and leg; progresses to bilateral arm flexion and extension with generalized clonic movements involving both arms and legs, head tilting, facial grimacing, intermittent mouth opening, and tongue protrusion. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. Additionally, brief bilateral arm movements with slight flexion and extension are observed, along with subtle rhythmic jerking of the right arm and hand; eyes remain closed during these episodes, without tonic stiffening, overt head version, facial automatisms, or generalized clonic activity.

Blank stare with initial rightward head version and right arm extension, while the left arm remains flexed at the elbow. Right arm extends further with slight hand elevation before returning to a flexed position. The episode progresses to bilateral tonic posturing, evolving into generalized tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of arms and legs, facial grimacing, and rhythmic opening and closing of the mouth. No clonic movements or automatisms are observed initially.

Initial blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements and facial grimacing.
Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion; progresses to bilateral arm flexion with rhythmic jerking, then evolves into generalized clonic movements.

Blank stare accompanied by rightward head version, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), with subsequent spread of rhythmic jerking to both limbs. Additional episodes feature blank stare with downward gaze, subtle head flexion, and slight shoulder elevation, without overt tonic or clonic movements. In another instance, patient opens eyes after initial eye closure, showing no tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, or automatisms; notable semi-purposeful behavior includes right hand moving to mouth, then to chest, and sustained placement on abdomen. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with eyes closed during motor activity and minimal limb motion in non-convulsive episodes.

Blank stare with subtle eye deviation to the right, accompanied by mild right arm flexion and left arm extension, without progression to clonic movements or generalized tonic–clonic activity. No overt seizure activity observed; patient remains seated upright, eyes open, looking downward, with no facial automatisms, head version, or limb movements, and no tonic or clonic signs present.
Patient exhibits a blank stare with subtle rightward head version; right arm flexion occurs followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. No tonic stiffening or automatisms observed.

No early signs of seizure activity observed. Patient remains seated with eyes open, hands resting on lap, and no limb movements or facial automatisms. At 26 seconds, patient brings both hands to head, adjusting the cap, then holds head with both hands for several seconds without facial or limb jerking.

Patient remains supine throughout the observation with variable eye status: initially with eyes closed and a blank stare accompanied by subtle rightward head version, right arm flexion, followed by bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks, during which the patient is unresponsive; subsequently, eyes are open with fixed gaze and no motor activity, automatisms, or seizure-like movements; later, eyes remain open with an alert appearance. No further seizure activity is observed, including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, limb posturing, automatisms, asynchronous movements, or changes in facial expression and limb posture. Respiratory pattern remains regular without apnea or gasping.

Initial blank stare with either rightward head version or upward eye deviation, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. Head is tilted back with eyes closed; arms may be raised and flexed at the elbows, hands clasped behind the head, or exhibit right arm extension with left arm flexion (fencer posturing). Progression to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms and legs, with sustained tonic stiffening of the trunk and slight rhythmic jerking of arms and trunk. Generalized muscular contractions occur without focal automatisms or lateralized posturing, and head thrusts backward during clonic phase. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with gradual relaxation but continued head extension at the end.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and subtle facial twitching. Initial presentation includes sudden bilateral arm flexion with head turning to the right. Movements cease without a visible postictal phase.
Blank stare with subtle facial grimacing; mild rhythmic jaw movement and occasional lip smacking. No clear limb movements or tonic–clonic activity observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare with subtle rightward head version; right arm flexion followed by left arm extension, progressing to bilateral rhythmic jerking. Patient exhibits a fixed, unresponsive gaze with subtle facial twitching; no overt limb movements or tonic-clonic activity are observed apart from the described arm movements and subsequent bilateral jerking. There are no automatisms noted, and the patient remains motionless except for the observed motor activity.

Blank stare accompanied by perioral twitching and subtle lip smacking, followed by asymmetric motor activity with right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), progressing to bilateral arm flexion and extension. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop symmetrically, involving limbs and trunk, with preserved eye opening and upward gaze.

Blank stare with subtle facial grimacing; right hand exhibits rhythmic flexion-extension movements, progressing to more pronounced jerking with mild facial twitching. No lateral head version or generalized tonic–clonic activity observed.

Blank stare, often with subtle rightward head version and/or eye deviation, accompanied by initial tonic stiffening or flexion of the right arm and occasional head turning to the right; progression to bilateral rhythmic jerking involving the right arm and face, with brief hand-to-face movement in some instances; one episode featured right arm flexion followed by bilateral arm extension without automatisms or head version.

Blank stare accompanied by variable manifestations including upward eye deviation, subtle facial twitching, jaw clenching, or minimal facial motion; in some instances, mild asynchronous jerking of the right hand and arm progresses to generalized clonic or tonic–clonic movements, while in others, no overt limb movement, automatisms, rhythmic jerking, head version, or asymmetric posturing is observed. Patient remains supine with eyes closed throughout all episodes.

Blank stare accompanied by upward eye deviation or gaze deviation, often with mouth opening and tongue protrusion or thrusting. Initial features include lip smacking, rightward head version, and right arm flexion. These may progress to rhythmic jerking involving the right arm and leg, evolving into bilateral tonic stiffening and generalized clonic movements. Bilateral arm flexion and rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs occur, with intermittent head turning to the right and sustained motor activity culminating in generalized tonic–clonic seizures.

Blank stare with rightward head version and concurrent right arm involvement (flexion or extension), progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Some episodes show initial asymmetric features such as fencer posture (right arm flexion with left arm extension) or unilateral arm flexion, which evolve into generalized motor activity. Episodes may include brief cessation and resumption of movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes closed and minimal movement post-event.

Blank stare accompanied by variable initial features including subtle or mild head version (rightward or leftward), asymmetric limb posturing (such as right arm extension with left arm flexion or vice versa—consistent with fencer posture), and focal motor signs like lip smacking, mouth opening, tongue protrusion, or jaw clenching. This evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerking involving all limbs, predominantly on the right side in some episodes. Automatisms such as intermittent mouth movements, tongue thrusting, and wide mouth opening are observed during the event. The patient remains unresponsive throughout each episode, with eyes either open or closed at onset and intermittently opening during the seizure. Progression to generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity is consistent across events, regardless of initial lateralizing features.

Blank stare with variable responsiveness, typically unresponsive throughout, occasionally with eyes open or closed, and mouth slightly agape or subtle jaw/mouth movements including lip smacking and perioral twitching. Common initial features include subtle rightward head version or deviation, sometimes leftward, often accompanied by right arm flexion or extension and contralateral arm posturing consistent with fencer posture (left arm extension with right arm flexion or vice versa). Progression frequently involves bilateral tonic stiffening of upper and lower limbs, evolving into rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms and legs, with occasional head turning to the right or generalized limb movements and facial grimacing. Some events remain focal, showing only right arm flexion and hand/forearm jerking without contralateral spread or generalization. A subset lacks overt tonic or clonic activity despite posturing and unresponsiveness. Events may end with sustained tonic posture or open mouth. Intermittent eye opening with upward gaze is observed in isolated instances.

- Blank stare accompanied by head version (rightward or mild leftward); right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture) observed in multiple episodes. - Progression to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs is consistently noted across active episodes. - Right arm extension noted in one episode, differing from predominant flexion pattern. - Automatisms are not observed during any event; patient remains unresponsive to external stimuli throughout all observed episodes. - One interval of observation shows no overt seizure activity: patient lies supine with eyes closed, no facial automatisms, limb jerking, tonic posturing, head version, cyanosis, or tongue biting; respiratory pattern is regular.

Blank stare, often accompanied by upward eye deviation, head version (rightward or leftward), head tilt, or subtle mouth movements, is the consistent initial feature. This evolves into focal motor signs including asymmetric arm posturing such as right or left arm flexion with contralateral extension (fencer posturing), unilateral arm tonic stiffening, or bilateral arm flexion. These focal features progress to generalized bilateral tonic stiffening of the arms, legs, and trunk, frequently with the head extended or turned. Tonic phase is followed by generalized rhythmic clonic jerking involving all limbs and trunk, often asynchronous, with repetitive shaking movements. Automatisms are common and include lip smacking, tongue thrusting, mouth opening, jaw clenching, facial grimacing, and intermittent vocalizations. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with eyes typically closed or deviated upward and the mouth open. No focal deficits or automatisms are observed in some events, but most include oral or facial automatisms concurrent with motor progression.

In some episodes, this includes rightward head version and right arm extension. Tonic phase evolves into bilateral clonic jerking of the limbs, including arms, legs, head, and neck, with rhythmic shaking that may become asynchronous. Facial muscle twitching and fixed gaze with eyes open are noted in some instances; automatisms and head version are absent in others. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases, which may culminate in sustained tonic posturing.

Facial grimacing and rhythmic opening and closing of the mouth are noted during some events. Jerking may occasionally present as asynchronous in the upper limbs without overt tonic–clonic activity or automatisms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the events. One observation showed no overt seizure activity, with the patient remaining supine, eyes closed, exhibiting no facial automatisms, head version, tonic or clonic movements, and limbs relaxed and motionless.
